heroOverview: Mansonelliasis is a group of filarial nematode infections caused by three related parasites — Mansonella perstans, M. streptocerca, and M. ozzardi — transmitted by biting midges (Culicoides species) or blackflies (Simulium species) in sub-Saharan Africa, Central and South America, and the Caribbean. Unlike the major pathogenic filariases, Mansonella species have long been regarded as relatively non-pathogenic commensals; however, accumulating evidence shows that M. perstans causes eosinophilia, urticaria, abdominal pain, and joint swelling, and that it resists standard filarial drugs including diethylcarbamazine, making treatment challenging. An estimated 114 million people are infected globally — making Mansonella perstans one of the most prevalent filarial parasites of humans — yet the infection remains profoundly understudied and frequently misdiagnosed.

keyStats.prevalence: An estimated 114 million people infected with Mansonella perstans globally (Wanji et al., 2015); M. ozzardi affects an estimated 5-13 million people in the Americas
keyStats.demographics: M. perstans: sub-Saharan Africa and South America; M. streptocerca: West and Central Africa; M. ozzardi: Central and South America, Caribbean
keyStats.avgAge: All ages affected; microfilaraemia increases with age in endemic populations due to cumulative exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Massively under-reported; prevalence surveys find M. perstans in 20-60% of adults in some hyperendemic rural African communities

definition: Mansonelliasis (ICD-10: B74.4) refers collectively to human filarial infections caused by three species of the genus Mansonella. Mansonella perstans, the most globally prevalent, is transmitted by Culicoides biting midges throughout sub-Saharan Africa and northern South America; adult worms inhabit the serous body cavities (pleural, peritoneal, pericardial) and release non-periodic microfilariae that circulate in blood. Mansonella streptocerca is a skin-dwelling filarial parasite transmitted by Culicoides midges in West and Central Africa, producing microfilariae in skin snips rather than blood. Mansonella ozzardi inhabits serous cavities in the thorax and abdomen and is transmitted by Culicoides and Simulium flies in the Americas; microfilariae circulate non-periodically in blood. All three species differ from the major pathogenic filariases (Wuchereria bancrofti, Brugia malayi, Loa loa) in that they generally produce milder clinical disease, but they cause substantial eosinophilia and a range of symptoms in a significant minority of infected individuals. Mansonelliasis is classified as a neglected tropical disease, and M. perstans in particular is a significant obstacle in mass drug administration programmes because it is resistant to ivermectin and diethylcarbamazine at standard doses. Infectious disease and tropical medicine specialists manage the condition.

typesClassification.description: The most common and widely distributed form; adult worms in serous cavities produce blood microfilariae and cause eosinophilia, urticaria, abdominal and pleuritic pain, Calabar-like swellings, and joint effusions; resists DEC and ivermectin.
A skin-dweller endemic to West and Central Africa; produces dermatitis with itching, pigmentation changes, and inguinal lymphadenopathy; microfilariae found in skin snips; responds to ivermectin.

Endemic to the Americas; causes headache, joint pain, pruritus, and urticaria; microfilariae circulate in blood and are non-periodic; often minimally symptomatic but contributes to eosinophilia in endemic communities.

primarySymptoms: Blood eosinophilia, consistently the most common laboratory finding across all three Mansonella species, often exceeding 1.5 × 10⁹/L and persisting for years without other obvious cause.
Urticaria and generalised pruritus, reflecting the immune response to circulating microfilariae and adult worm antigens.
Abdominal pain and bloating from peritoneal irritation by adult M. perstans worms inhabiting the abdominal serous cavity.
Pleuritic chest pain from adult worms in the pleural cavity, occurring intermittently and sometimes mimicking pleuritis or pericarditis.
Transient joint swelling and arthralgia — Calabar-like swellings — most often affecting the wrists and ankles in M. perstans infection.
Headache, dizziness, and visual disturbances in M. ozzardi infection, a distinctive clinical feature reported particularly in Amazonian studies.
Dermatitis with intense pruritus and hypopigmented or hyperpigmented skin macules in M. streptocerca infection, resembling leprosy or pityriasis versicolor.
Angioedema — transient localised subcutaneous swelling similar to Calabar swellings — in a subset of M. perstans-infected individuals.
Fatigue and malaise during periods of high microfilaraemia, particularly in patients with coexisting nutritional deficits in endemic settings.
Lymphadenopathy in the inguinal region in M. streptocerca infection from adult worm migration in the skin and dermis.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained persistent blood eosinophilia above 1.0 × 10⁹/L in a patient who has lived in or recently returned from sub-Saharan Africa or South America.
Recurrent generalised urticaria without an obvious allergic trigger in a person from an endemic area.
Episodic abdominal or pleuritic pain without a structural cause on imaging in a traveller from Central Africa.
emergencySigns: Pericardial effusion causing haemodynamic compromise (cardiac tamponade features) — rare but documented from M. perstans in the pericardial sac.
Hypereosinophilic syndrome with organ damage (cardiac or neurological) if extreme eosinophilia from Mansonella triggers end-organ injury.
Severe allergic encephalopathy if misidentified as loiasis and treated with high-dose ivermectin in a patient who has co-infection with high Loa loa microfilaraemia.

causes.cause: Bite of infected Culicoides biting midges transmitting M. perstans and M. streptocerca
causes.description: Culicoides midges are tiny (1-2 mm) biting flies that feed at dusk and dawn. Infected midges deposit infective L3 larvae on the skin during feeding. The larvae penetrate and mature to adults: M. perstans worms reside in serous cavities and M. streptocerca adults migrate in the skin. Adult female worms are long-lived, releasing microfilariae into blood or skin for years.

causes.cause: Bite of Culicoides or Simulium blackflies transmitting M. ozzardi in the Americas
causes.description: M. ozzardi is transmitted by both Culicoides and Simulium species depending on geographic location within the Americas. Adult worms inhabit the thoracic and abdominal serous cavities; microfilariae circulate non-periodically in blood and are taken up during the vector's blood meal.

causes.cause: Long-lived adult worms producing persistent microfilaraemia without treatment
causes.description: Adult Mansonella worms live for many years in human tissue, continuously producing microfilariae. Without treatment, microfilaraemia persists indefinitely, sustaining the immune stimulation that drives eosinophilia and clinical symptoms.

causes.cause: Immune-mediated inflammatory response to microfilarial antigens
causes.description: The clinical symptoms of mansonelliasis — eosinophilia, urticaria, joint swelling, serosal pain — are driven primarily by the host's immune response to microfilarial surface antigens and worm-derived molecules such as phosphorylcholine, rather than by direct worm invasion of vital organs.

causes.cause: Wolbachia endosymbiont bacteria within Mansonella worms
causes.description: Like other major filarial nematodes, Mansonella species harbour Wolbachia intracellular bacteria. These endosymbionts are essential for worm fertility and survival; they also stimulate host innate immune responses that contribute to tissue inflammation and may represent a treatment target via doxycycline.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to sub-Saharan Africa (M. perstans, M. streptocerca)
riskFactors.description: M. perstans is highly prevalent throughout the forest and savannah zones of sub-Saharan Africa, particularly in DRC, Central African Republic, Gabon, Cameroon, Nigeria, and Uganda. Local Culicoides vector density drives prevalence.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to Central and South America and the Caribbean (M. ozzardi)
riskFactors.description: M. ozzardi is endemic from the Yucatan to Argentina, with highest burden in Amazonian communities in Brazil and Colombia, the Caribbean islands, and parts of Venezuela.

riskFactors.factor: Outdoor exposure at dawn and dusk in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Culicoides midges are active at low-light periods; outdoor workers, farmers, and people who sleep in open or poorly screened dwellings have greatest exposure and highest microfilaraemia densities.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of biting midge control measures
riskFactors.description: Fine-mesh bednets that exclude Culicoides (mesh size <0.5 mm), insect repellents, and closed window screens reduce exposure; standard mosquito nets do not exclude the tiny Culicoides midges.

riskFactors.factor: Age and duration of residence in endemic area
riskFactors.description: Microfilaraemia prevalence and density increase with age in endemic populations; older adults have had more cumulative exposure and are more likely to carry high worm burdens with persistent eosinophilia.

riskFactors.factor: Coexistence with other filarial infections (loiasis, onchocerciasis)
riskFactors.description: Mansonella species share endemic zones with Loa loa and Onchocerca volvulus in Central Africa; co-infection is common and complicates both diagnosis and treatment, as misidentifying Mansonella microfilariae as Loa loa could lead to inappropriate drug dosing.

affectedDemographics: Rural agricultural and forest communities across sub-Saharan Africa, especially DRC, CAR, Gabon, Cameroon, and Uganda
Amazon basin communities in Brazil, Colombia, Peru, and Venezuela (M. ozzardi)
Caribbean island populations in Martinique, Guadeloupe, Trinidad, and parts of the Lesser Antilles (M. ozzardi)
Travellers and expatriates with prolonged exposure in hyperendemic areas, who may present to tropical medicine clinics on return
People co-infected with other filarial parasites (loiasis, onchocerciasis) in overlapping endemic zones
diagnosisOverview: Mansonelliasis is diagnosed by identifying Mansonella microfilariae in blood or skin samples under microscopy. For M. perstans and M. ozzardi, a thick blood smear is collected; unlike Loa loa, Mansonella microfilariae are non-periodic and can be collected at any time of day. The microfilariae of M. perstans are characterised by their small size, blunt tail, and absence of a sheath, distinguishing them from Loa loa and Wuchereria microfilariae on morphological examination by an experienced microscopist. M. streptocerca microfilariae are found in skin snips taken from the buttock, shoulder, or calf rather than blood; they are distinguished from Onchocerca volvulus by tail morphology and absence of sheath. Blood eosinophilia is consistently elevated across all three species and supports the diagnosis in an endemic-area patient. Species-specific PCR assays have been developed for research purposes and are available at some reference laboratories; they are more sensitive than morphology-based blood smear examination and can distinguish species in co-endemic areas. Serological tests lack the specificity to distinguish Mansonella from other filarial infections reliably. Imaging has no diagnostic role, though incidental peritoneal or pericardial findings may be noted. The main differential diagnoses are loiasis, onchocerciasis, strongyloidiasis, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (formerly Churg-Strauss), all of which cause eosinophilia and systemic symptoms.

diagnosticTests.test: Thick blood smear (any time of day) for M. perstans and M. ozzardi
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and morphologically characterises Mansonella microfilariae in peripheral blood; non-periodicity means sampling time is not critical, unlike Loa loa or W. bancrofti.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw followed by preparation of a thick smear and Giemsa staining; read by an experienced microscopist at a tropical disease laboratory. Results within hours to 1 day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects M. streptocerca microfilariae in the dermis; distinguishes this species from Onchocerca volvulus, with which it shares the same skin-snip sampling method and geographic range.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small superficial skin shave biopsy of a few millimetres is taken (typically from the buttock or shoulder) under brief local anaesthesia; the sample is incubated in saline and emerging microfilariae identified microscopically.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with differential (eosinophil count)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies blood eosinophilia, which is almost universally elevated in mansonelliasis and is the most common laboratory clue prompting parasitic investigation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; eosinophilia above 1.0 × 10⁹/L is expected. Results available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms Mansonella species in ambiguous cases and distinguishes co-infections in areas where multiple filarial species overlap; more sensitive than morphology for low-level microfilaraemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample or skin snip sent to a specialist reference laboratory; turnaround varies from days to weeks depending on the centre.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for pericardial effusion in the rare event of M. perstans involvement of the pericardium, and screens for eosinophilic myocardial damage in patients with extreme eosinophilia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive bedside ultrasound examination taking 20-30 minutes; no radiation, no contrast required.

treatmentOverview: Mansonelliasis is one of the more challenging filarial infections to treat because M. perstans — the most prevalent species — is intrinsically resistant to diethylcarbamazine (DEC) and ivermectin at standard doses, the two drugs most effective against other filarial infections. Doxycycline, which targets the Wolbachia endosymbiont essential for worm survival, has emerged as the most effective treatment for M. perstans, with a course of 200 mg daily for 6 weeks achieving significant microfilarial clearance and even macrofilaricidal effects in randomised trials. Microfilaraemia may take 12-24 months to decline fully after doxycycline, reflecting the slow action of Wolbachia depletion on worm fertility and longevity. Moxidectin, a novel macrocyclic lactone with greater potency than ivermectin, has shown activity against M. perstans in early clinical data and may become a treatment option as evidence accumulates. For M. streptocerca, ivermectin at a single dose of 150 µg/kg produces marked microfilarial reduction and is the drug of choice; a second dose may be needed for complete clearance. For M. ozzardi, ivermectin similarly reduces microfilaraemia significantly. DEC is not effective for M. ozzardi or M. streptocerca and should be avoided. Symptom management — antihistamines for urticaria, NSAIDs for joint pain — helps during the infection period. Treatment success is monitored by repeat microfilarial counts at 6-12 months post-treatment. Given the persistence of infection and the slow response to treatment, patient counselling about realistic timelines is essential.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (for M. perstans)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-Wolbachia therapy; doxycycline 200 mg daily for 6 weeks depletes the Wolbachia endosymbiont required for worm fertility. Microfilaraemia declines over 12-24 months post-treatment; macrofilaricidal activity reported in a randomised trial.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show approximately 70-90% reduction in M. perstans microfilaraemia 12 months post-treatment; the most effective regimen currently available for this species.

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin (for M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi)
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of 150 µg/kg is the standard treatment for M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi; it kills microfilariae and reduces symptoms. Repeat dosing may be needed for complete clearance. Not effective for M. perstans.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves approximately 70-85% reduction in microfilaraemia for M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi after one or two doses.

medicalTreatments.name: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC)
medicalTreatments.description: Not effective against M. perstans or M. ozzardi at standard doses; historically used but abandoned due to lack of efficacy and significant side effects without benefit. May have partial activity against M. streptocerca when ivermectin is unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate for M. perstans below 20% at standard doses; not recommended as primary therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Antihistamines and NSAIDs (symptomatic)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine or loratadine) control urticaria and pruritus; NSAIDs manage joint pain and pleuritic symptoms during the active infection period.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control achieved in approximately 70-80% of patients with urticaria and joint pain; no effect on parasite burden.

alternativeTreatments.name: Fine-mesh bednets and Culicoides exclusion
alternativeTreatments.description: Standard mosquito nets (mesh 1.5 mm) do not exclude Culicoides midges, which are much smaller. Fine-mesh nets (<0.5 mm) impregnated with permethrin significantly reduce midge bites and represent the most practical personal protection method.

alternativeTreatments.name: Insect repellents containing DEET or picaridin
alternativeTreatments.description: Applied to exposed skin during active midge hours (dusk and dawn) in endemic areas, DEET-based repellents reduce landing rates of Culicoides midges; their full efficacy against these tiny flies is lower than against mosquitoes but remains useful.

whySeeSpecialist: Mansonelliasis requires a tropical medicine or infectious disease specialist for two key reasons: correct species identification (which determines drug choice) requires microscopist expertise and sometimes PCR not available in general practice; and the treatment choice must account for the possibility of co-infection with Loa loa, since misdiagnosing Mansonella microfilariae as Loa loa or vice versa can lead to either inappropriate withholding or dangerous administration of ivermectin. The chronic nature of the infection and slow treatment response also benefit from specialist follow-up with serial microfilarial counts.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek care at a tropical medicine centre with experienced parasitology microscopists and access to filarial PCR. In the UK, the Hospital for Tropical Diseases and Liverpool School of Tropical Medicine handle rare filarial infections. In continental Europe, the Institute of Tropical Medicine in Antwerp is a reference centre. In the United States, the NIH Clinical Center's Laboratory of Parasitic Diseases and academic tropical medicine programmes diagnose and manage imported mansonelliasis. Confirm the centre can perform species-specific microfilarial identification and blood Loa loa co-infection screening.
preventionStrategies: Use fine-mesh (<0.5 mm) insect nets impregnated with permethrin when sleeping in endemic areas to exclude Culicoides midges.
Apply DEET-based insect repellents to exposed skin during dusk and dawn outdoor activities in endemic regions.
Wear long-sleeved shirts and trousers treated with permethrin when outdoors at midge-active hours.
Support community-level Culicoides vector control programmes where available.
Screen for Mansonella co-infection before initiating ivermectin-based mass drug administration in Central or West Africa.
Avoid unnecessary outdoor exposure during peak midge activity periods in hyperendemic areas.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full doxycycline course (6 weeks) without interruption; doxycycline works slowly by depleting Wolbachia, and incomplete courses are ineffective.
Take doxycycline with a full glass of water and remain upright for 30 minutes to reduce oesophageal irritation.
Use high-factor sunscreen and wear protective clothing during doxycycline treatment due to photosensitivity.
Attend follow-up microfilarial blood smear appointments at 6 and 12 months after treatment to confirm clearance.
Inform any future treating clinicians of your history of filarial infection and treatment before receiving any macrolide anti-parasitic drugs.

recommended: Take doxycycline with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects — a meal without dairy products immediately before the dose
Adequate protein and caloric intake to support immune function during prolonged infection and treatment
Oral rehydration if doxycycline causes gastrointestinal side effects such as nausea or diarrhoea
avoid: Dairy products, antacids, iron supplements, and calcium-containing foods within 2 hours of a doxycycline dose, as they impair absorption
Alcohol during doxycycline therapy as it may reduce drug levels and increase gastrointestinal side effects

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity need not be restricted during mansonelliasis. During periods of pleuritic chest pain or active joint swelling, heavy exertion may worsen discomfort; moderate activity with rest when symptomatic is advised. Doxycycline-related photosensitivity means outdoor exercise in strong sunlight should be accompanied by protective clothing and sunscreen. Normal exercise levels can be fully maintained once microfilarial clearance is confirmed.
dailyManagement: Take doxycycline at the same time each day, with food and a full glass of water; use a phone reminder for the 6-week course.
Track urticaria and skin symptom episodes in a diary to monitor improvement over the treatment and follow-up period.
Keep antihistamines accessible for pruritus and urticaria breakthrough during treatment.
Return for the scheduled 6-month and 12-month blood smears to confirm microfilarial clearance — do not assume cure from symptom resolution alone.
Report any new chest pain, significant breathlessness, or joint effusion to your clinician promptly, as rare pericardial or joint complications need imaging assessment.
prognosis: The overall prognosis for mansonelliasis is favourable with correct species identification and appropriate treatment. M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi respond well to ivermectin, with microfilarial clearance in the majority of treated patients and resolution of skin and systemic symptoms. M. perstans has historically been more difficult to treat, but doxycycline-based Wolbachia depletion achieves significant microfilarial clearance in 70-90% of treated patients, with symptom resolution over 12-24 months. Untreated infection persists for years but rarely progresses to severe organ damage in otherwise healthy individuals; the main consequences are chronic eosinophilia, recurrent urticaria, and quality-of-life impairment. Pericardial or extreme eosinophilic complications are rare. Permanent residual disease after successful treatment is uncommon, and most patients return to full health.
recoveryTimeline: For M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi treated with ivermectin, microfilaraemia decreases within 2-4 weeks of treatment and symptoms improve over 1-3 months. A follow-up skin snip or blood smear at 3 months confirms clearance. For M. perstans treated with doxycycline, clinical improvement (reduced urticaria, joint pain) can be noted within 4-8 weeks, but microfilarial counts decline slowly over 12-24 months as the Wolbachia-depleted worms lose fertility and die. Eosinophilia normalises over 6-12 months after successful treatment. Repeat doxycycline courses may be needed if microfilaraemia persists at 12-month review.
complications: Chronic persistent eosinophilia — sustained elevation for years in untreated cases, occasionally triggering secondary organ effects.
Pericardial effusion from adult M. perstans in the pericardial sac — rare but can cause chest pain and, in extreme cases, haemodynamic compromise.
Hypereosinophilic syndrome in patients with very high eosinophil counts, potentially causing cardiac or neurological end-organ damage.
Diagnostic misidentification as loiasis leading to inappropriate ivermectin treatment in patients with concurrent high Loa loa microfilaraemia and consequent encephalopathy risk.
Prolonged skin disease and hypopigmentation or hyperpigmentation in untreated M. streptocerca infection that may be cosmetically and psychologically distressing.
Treatment failure from suboptimal antibiotic adherence with doxycycline, leading to persistent infection and symptom recurrence.

supportResources.name: NIH NIAID — Parasitic Diseases resources

confusedWithConditions.name: Loiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Loiasis is caused by Loa loa and produces dramatic Calabar swellings and visible eye-crossing worms that mansonelliasis does not cause; blood microfilariae of Loa loa have a midday peak and a distinctive morphology, requiring expert microscopist differentiation from Mansonella.

confusedWithConditions.name: Onchocerciasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: M. streptocerca and Onchocerca volvulus both cause skin-snip-detectable microfilariae; they are distinguished by microfilarial tail morphology and the associated nodules and eye disease of onchocerciasis that do not occur in M. streptocerca.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eosinophilia (other causes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eosinophilia from mansonelliasis must be distinguished from other tropical causes (strongyloidiasis, toxocariasis, allergic disease); blood microfilarial identification is required to confirm the filarial origin.

relatedConditions.name: Loiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are filarial infections with overlapping endemic zones in Central Africa; they co-occur in patients and have critical diagnostic implications for drug safety — confusing Mansonella with Loa loa microfilariae can lead to dangerous treatment decisions.

relatedConditions.name: Onchocerciasis
relatedConditions.relevance: M. streptocerca shares the skin-snip diagnostic method and Central African rainforest distribution with Onchocerca volvulus; they are frequently co-endemic and co-infect the same individuals.

relatedConditions.name: Filariasis due to Wuchereria bancrofti
relatedConditions.relevance: Lymphatic filariasis and mansonelliasis are co-endemic across sub-Saharan Africa; Mansonella perstans resistance to ivermectin complicates mass drug administration campaigns for lymphatic filariasis eradication.

relatedConditions.name: Eosinophilia
relatedConditions.relevance: Mansonelliasis is a leading cause of persistent unexplained blood eosinophilia in travellers and residents returning from endemic regions, making it central to the tropical eosinophilia differential diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: Mansonelliasis is a group of parasitic worm infections caused by three Mansonella filarial nematodes — M. perstans, M. streptocerca, and M. ozzardi — transmitted by biting midges in Africa and South America. It is one of the most prevalent but least-known filarial infections affecting humans.

faqs.question: How many people are infected with Mansonella?
faqs.answer: An estimated 114 million people are infected with Mansonella perstans globally, making it one of the most common filarial parasites of humans. M. ozzardi infects approximately 5 to 13 million people in the Americas.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: The most consistent symptom is high blood eosinophilia. Other symptoms include urticaria, pruritus, abdominal and pleuritic pain, joint swelling, and skin rash with pigment changes. Many people in endemic areas have microfilariae with few or no symptoms.

faqs.question: How is mansonelliasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by identifying Mansonella microfilariae in a blood smear (M. perstans and M. ozzardi) or skin snip (M. streptocerca) under microscopy. Blood can be sampled at any time of day since Mansonella microfilariae are non-periodic.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for Mansonella perstans?
faqs.answer: Doxycycline 200 mg daily for 6 weeks, which targets the Wolbachia bacteria inside the worm, is the most effective treatment for M. perstans. Microfilaraemia declines slowly over 12 to 24 months after treatment.

faqs.question: Why is Mansonella perstans hard to treat?
faqs.answer: M. perstans is resistant to the standard filarial drugs diethylcarbamazine and ivermectin at normal doses. Doxycycline, which kills the Wolbachia endosymbiont the worm depends on, is currently the most effective option.

faqs.question: Does ivermectin treat mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: Ivermectin works for M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi but is ineffective for M. perstans. A single dose of 150 µg/kg is used for the two ivermectin-sensitive species and achieves microfilarial clearance in most cases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between M. perstans and loiasis?
faqs.answer: Both cause eosinophilia and subcutaneous swellings in Central Africa, but loiasis produces dramatic Calabar swellings and a visible worm crossing the eye, which M. perstans does not. Blood microfilariae look different under microscopy and require expert differentiation.

faqs.question: Where is mansonelliasis found?
faqs.answer: M. perstans and M. streptocerca are found in sub-Saharan Africa, particularly DRC, CAR, Gabon, Cameroon, and Nigeria. M. ozzardi is found in Central and South America and the Caribbean, including the Amazon basin and parts of the Caribbean islands.

faqs.question: How is Mansonella transmitted?
faqs.answer: All three Mansonella species are transmitted by biting midges of the genus Culicoides. M. ozzardi is also transmitted by Simulium blackflies in some areas. The midges are tiny, day and dusk-biting flies active in forest and savannah environments.

faqs.question: Is mansonelliasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most people, mansonelliasis causes persistent eosinophilia and troublesome but non-life-threatening symptoms like urticaria and abdominal pain. Pericardial or hypereosinophilic complications are rare but can be serious.

faqs.question: Can mansonelliasis affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Adult M. perstans can inhabit the pericardial sac and rarely cause pericardial effusion. Extreme eosinophilia from the infection has been associated with eosinophilic cardiac disease in a minority of cases, but serious cardiac complications are uncommon.

faqs.question: How long does treatment take for mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: Doxycycline for M. perstans is given for 6 weeks, but microfilaraemia declines slowly over 12 to 24 months after the course ends. Ivermectin for M. streptocerca or M. ozzardi works faster, with microfilarial clearance over 2 to 4 weeks.

faqs.question: Does mansonelliasis cause skin problems?
faqs.answer: M. streptocerca causes dermatitis with intense itching and patchy skin pigment changes that can resemble leprosy. M. perstans can cause urticaria and angioedema. M. ozzardi causes urticaria and pruritus in some patients.

faqs.question: Can mansonelliasis be confused with strongyloidiasis?
faqs.answer: Both cause persistent eosinophilia and abdominal symptoms in travellers from tropical regions, but strongyloidiasis has a different life cycle (skin penetration, not insect bite) and is diagnosed by stool examination or serology rather than blood smear.

faqs.question: Is mansonelliasis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes. M. streptocerca and M. ozzardi respond well to ivermectin and are curable. M. perstans is treatable with doxycycline, which achieves microfilarial clearance in approximately 70 to 90 percent of patients over 12 to 24 months.

faqs.question: How do you prevent mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: Prevent mansonelliasis by using fine-mesh insect nets (under 0.5 mm) and DEET-based repellents during active midge hours, particularly at dusk and dawn in Central African or South American endemic areas.

faqs.question: Why does mansonelliasis cause eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: The immune system responds to microfilarial antigens and worm-derived molecules by activating eosinophils, a normal anti-parasitic response. Eosinophilia persists as long as live worms and microfilariae are present and normalises after successful treatment.

faqs.question: What is Wolbachia and why does it matter in mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: Wolbachia are bacteria that live inside Mansonella worms and are essential for worm fertility and survival. Doxycycline kills these bacteria, indirectly sterilising and eventually killing the worms. This is why doxycycline works for M. perstans when standard filarial drugs do not.

faqs.question: What specialist should I see for mansonelliasis?
faqs.answer: Seek a tropical medicine or infectious disease specialist with access to expert filarial microscopists and PCR testing. National reference centres for tropical diseases in the UK, Europe, and USA handle imported mansonelliasis and can differentiate species safely before treatment.

metaDescription: Mansonelliasis is a filarial worm infection causing eosinophilia, urticaria and abdominal pain in Africa and South America. Learn about diagnosis and treatment.
